artifacts.summary: Complete custom yank ring architecture design with production-quality code sketches for 6 modules, file change list, comparison analysis, and risk assessment

memory_candidates.content: yanky.nvim with system_clipboard.sync_with_ring = true causes Neovim to freeze on Wayland/GNOME after system sleep or window resize. The FocusGained event triggers a blocking wl-paste call via systemlist() which hangs when the Wayland clipboard state is stale. Fix: set sync_with_ring = false.

Neovim :mode command re-sends all terminal mode escape sequences. Combined with :redraw! and treesitter parser:invalidate()/parse(), this provides comprehensive TUI state recovery after terminal corruption.
WezTerm sends FocusGained/FocusLost terminal escape sequences only to the active pane in the active tab. Background tabs never receive focus events.

vim.fn.getreg(+) on Wayland calls wl-paste synchronously and can hang indefinitely after system sleep (Neovim issue #24470, unresolved). The fix is to use vim.system with timeout for non-blocking reads, or define a custom vim.g.clipboard provider.

yanky.nvim system_clipboard module creates FocusGained autocmd that calls vim.fn.getreg(+) to detect external clipboard changes. PR #117 prevents loops but does NOT add timeout. Only safe mitigation is sync_with_ring=false or replacing getreg with vim.system()+timeout.

vim.system() in Neovim 0.10+ provides non-blocking subprocess execution with built-in timeout support. Pattern: vim.system({"wl-paste","--no-newline"},{text=true,timeout=2000},function(obj) vim.schedule(function() if obj.code==0 then use(obj.stdout) end end) end)

Post-sleep rendering recovery in Neovim requires: (1) vim.cmd("mode") to reset terminal state, (2) vim.cmd("redraw!") for full screen redraw, (3) treesitter parser:invalidate(true)+parse() to regenerate syntax highlighting. Must run on FocusGained/VimResume autocommands.

memory_candidates.content: OpenCode permission system uses SSE event permission.asked with properties {id, sessionID, permission, patterns, metadata, always, tool?}. Replies via POST /permission/:requestID/reply with body {reply: once|always|reject, message?: string}. Pending permissions listed via GET /permission.
Nextcord persistent views (buttons that survive bot restarts) require: timeout=None in View.__init__, unique custom_id on each button, and re-registration via bot.add_view() on startup.
When implementing persistent Discord button views that need unique identifiers (like permission request IDs), use dynamic button creation with custom_id encoding rather than decorator-based buttons.

memory_candidates.content: OpenCode sessions can be killed via two distinct paths: explicit kill (POST /kill via the Discord bot API where the bot is the caller with access to thread_id) and implicit kill (SSE session.deleted/session.error events or TUI process death where the bot is only an observer). The SSE event stream emits session.deleted and session.error events that signal session termination. When implementing cleanup for any session-based relay system, both paths must be handled separately since the bot's access to session metadata differs between them.

Discord thread cleanup for bot-created threads can be done via Thread.edit(archived=True, locked=True) for non-destructive archival or Thread.delete() for permanent removal. Archiving is the safer default since it preserves conversation history and is reversible. Threads created as PUBLIC_THREAD with auto_archive_duration will auto-archive after the configured period of inactivity, but explicit cleanup provides immediate housekeeping rather than waiting for the timeout.

OpenCode sessions can be killed via two distinct paths: explicit kill (POST /kill via the Discord bot API where the bot is the caller with access to thread_id) and implicit kill (SSE session.deleted/session.error events or TUI process death where the bot is only an observer).
Discord thread cleanup for bot-created threads can be done via Thread.edit(archived=True, locked=True) for non-destructive archival or Thread.delete() for permanent removal.

artifacts.summary: Complete analysis of session kill lifecycle with two distinct paths: explicit (POST /kill with bot as caller) and implicit (SSE session.deleted/error events or TUI death). Identifies the exact insertion point for thread cleanup in _handle_kill(), missing SSE event handlers in TuiSseSubscriber, and recommends a health-check polling loop as a fallback. Recommends thread archiving+locking as default cleanup mode.

5-phase implementation plan for auto-cleanup of killed OpenCode sessions from Discord. Covers explicit kill path (api.py), implicit kill path via SSE events (sse_subscriber.py), health-check polling loop (bot.py), and configurable cleanup mode (config.py).
Implementation summary for Task 57: Auto cleanup killed sessions from Discord covering explicit kill path, implicit SSE event handlers, and health-check polling loop.

completed_projects.completion_summary: Implemented auto-cleanup of killed OpenCode sessions from Discord across all three kill paths: explicit kill via POST /kill (api.py), implicit kill via SSE session.deleted/session.error events (sse_subscriber.py), and stale session detection via a periodic health-check polling loop (bot.py). Added configurable cleanup mode (archive vs delete) via OPENCODE_DISCORD_CLEANUP_MODE env var.

completed_projects.description: Implement the reverse relay for the Discord-OpenCode bot: when a user types in the OpenCode TUI and receives an assistant response, that response should automatically appear in the corresponding linked Discord thread. The existing Discord->OpenCode relay is fully operational; this task adds the missing TUI->Discord direction by introducing an SSE subscriber that listens to the TUI's /event endpoint and posts assistant responses back to Discord.

artifacts.summary: Research findings for Task 56: Bidirectional Discord-OpenCode Relay. Analyzed existing Discord->OpenCode relay, OpenCode SSE event API, live server behavior, deduplication challenge, and recommended pure aiohttp SSE parsing approach.
Implementation plan with 4 phases (5 hours total) to create TuiSseSubscriber class, integrate subscriber lifecycle into bot.py, wire link/kill API endpoints, and test end-to-end behavior.

completed_projects.completion_summary: Implemented the reverse relay for the Discord-OpenCode bot by creating a new TuiSseSubscriber class (sse_subscriber.py) that listens to the TUI SSE event stream and posts assistant responses back to Discord. Integrated subscriber lifecycle into bot.py with dedup guard and health checks, and wired link/kill endpoints in api.py to start/stop subscribers automatically.

memory_candidates.content: When implementing SSE subscribers with asyncio, store asyncio.Task objects in a dict keyed by session_id for lightweight management. Add dead-task cleanup (check task.done()) before starting new subscribers to prevent crashed tasks from blocking restarts. Cancel and await tasks in close() to avoid Task exception was never retrieved warnings.

For bidirectional relay deduplication between Discord→OpenCode and TUI→Discord paths, use a shared session-level guard set (e.g., _discord_relay_sessions) that is populated before the Discord relay starts and cleared only after the response is posted back to Discord. This blocks the reverse SSE subscriber from posting duplicates during the round-trip window.

When parsing Server-Sent Events (SSE) with aiohttp, use response.content.readline() in a loop rather than iterating over chunks. This cleanly handles line-oriented SSE format (data: {...}) and properly detects connection closure (empty line). Wrap readline in try/except to handle CancelledError and connection errors gracefully.

memory_candidates.content: OpenCode has emerged as a new major memory consumer (~1.7 GB across 3 instances), comparable to Claude Code. The existing claude-memory-tracker only matches claude processes, making OpenCode invisible to process-specific memory tracking. Future memory monitoring should track both Claude and OpenCode, or use a broader pattern like AI assistant process matching.

On 2026-03-29 the system experienced a critical memory spike to 99% with swap at 86%, yet recovered fully without earlyoom intervention. This validates that the zram + swapfile + VM tuning infrastructure from Task 39 is resilient even under extreme pressure. The system can handle 99% memory utilization without hard failure.

When extending process monitoring scripts in NixOS/Home Manager, it is often simpler to expand an existing pgrep pattern rather than create a new service. This preserves systemd unit names, log files, and CSV schemas while minimizing migration risk.

For NixOS flake configurations, home-manager switch --flake /path#user can be run without sudo and will pick up home.nix changes, but nixos-rebuild switch requires sudo and may need manual execution if the agent lacks password access.

completed_projects.completion_summary: Extended claude-memory-tracker pgrep pattern to include OpenCode processes and added opencode to the earlyoom --prefer list. Rebuilt Home Manager configuration, restarted monitoring services, and validated that OpenCode entries now appear in the CSV log. NixOS rebuild for earlyoom requires manual sudo execution.
roadmap_items: Review memory logs and design system optimizations
